schools.summary: The historical figure (Genesis 14:18-20): 'this Melchisedec, king of Salem (probably Jerusalem, or a city of that name), priest of the most high God' — a king who was also a priest, uniting both offices, and a priest of the true God outside the line of Israel; 'who met Abraham returning from the slaughter of the kings (the defeat of the four kings, Genesis 14), and blessed him' — coming out to meet the victorious patriarch and pronouncing a blessing on him (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Chrysostom: this brief, mysterious appearance of a king-priest of the true God, blessing Abraham, was designed by the Spirit as a type of Christ; the writer takes up the figure to prove the superiority of Christ's royal priesthood over the Levitical.

themes: this Melchisedec, king of Salem, priest of the most high God
who met Abraham returning from the slaughter of the kings, and blessed him
Melchizedek the king-priest who blessed Abraham
a type of Christ's royal priesthood

schools.summary: The significant act: 'to whom also Abraham gave a tenth part of all (the spoils of his victory)' — the patriarch paying tithes to Melchizedek, an act of homage acknowledging his superior dignity (developed in vv.4-10); 'first being by interpretation King of righteousness (melek-tsedek)' — his very name signifying righteousness, a fitting type of Christ the true King of righteousness; 'and after that also King of Salem (shalem), which is, King of peace' — his title signifying peace, the order being righteousness first then peace, as in Christ who first establishes righteousness and then peace (Romans 5:1; Isaiah 32:17) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: to whom also Abraham gave a tenth part of all
first being by interpretation King of righteousness, and after that also King of Salem, which is, King of peace
the tithe Abraham paid and the meaning of the names
King of righteousness and peace, typifying Christ

schools.summary: Barnes, Robertson, and Ellicott (with Calvin and Gill) hold that 'without father, without mother, without descent (agenealogetos — without genealogy), having neither beginning of days nor end of life' describes not Melchizedek's actual being but the silence of the Scripture record: he was a real man who had parents and was born and died, but Scripture (which carefully records the Levitical genealogies, births, and deaths) mentions none of these for him, so that in the record he stands alone, appearing suddenly as a perpetual priest. The argument is from the silence of the text (made much of by Philo, not to be pressed). 'Made like (aphomoiomenos — formed as a copy, a type) unto the Son of God; abideth a priest continually' — a divinely-ordained type of Christ's perpetual priesthood; the phrase 'made like unto the Son of God' itself proving he was not the Son, since one cannot be 'made like' oneself (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: Melchizedek is not considered in his private capacity but as a sacred type of Christ; the Spirit designedly omitted his birth, kindred, and death, that he might shadow forth one above the common order of men, in whom we see (as in a mirror) that Christ has neither beginning nor end. Calvin sharply rejects 'the delirious notions of those who dream that Christ himself, or the holy Spirit, or an angel appeared,' and finds 'no probability' in the conjecture (Jewish tradition) that Melchizedek was Shem the son of Noah, since to make him a known individual would destroy the likeness.

Cyril cites Daniel 7's 'Ancient of days' and 'one like the Son of Man'; the patristic instinct sees in Melchizedek's mysterious, fatherless presentation a shadow of the eternal Son — though the consensus holds this lies in the record's silence, not in Melchizedek's nature (the Melchizedekian sect that made him the Son, Spirit, or an angel being rejected).

debates.question: What is the nature of Melchizedek ('without father... without end of life')?

sides.position: A real man, presented by the silence of the Scripture record as a type of Christ's eternal priesthood
A superhuman being — the Son of God himself, the Holy Spirit, or an angel (the Melchizedekians)
holders: the third-century Melchizedekian sect (rejected by Calvin as 'delirious')

sides.position: Shem the son of Noah (Jewish tradition)

themes: Without father, without mother, without descent, having neither beginning of days, nor end of life
but made like unto the Son of God; abideth a priest continually
Melchizedek without recorded genealogy, a type of the Son
the crux of Melchizedek's nature

schools.summary: The writer's appeal: 'now consider (behold, ponder) how great this man was, unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of the spoils' — the greatness of Melchizedek proved by the fact that Abraham, the venerated patriarch (the great father of the nation, the friend of God, to whom the promises were given), paid him tithes; to pay a tenth was an act of homage acknowledging a superior, so Abraham's tithe shows Melchizedek's surpassing dignity — the foundation of the argument that Melchizedek (and so Christ's priesthood) is greater than the Levitical (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

themes: consider how great this man was, unto whom even the patriarch Abraham gave the tenth of the spoils
the greatness of Melchizedek
Abraham's tithe homage to a superior
greater than the patriarch

schools.summary: The contrast being set up: 'and verily they that are of the sons of Levi, who receive the office of the priesthood, have a commandment to take tithes of the people according to the law' — the Levitical priests having a legal right to receive tithes from the people of Israel; 'that is, of their brethren (fellow-Israelites, their own kinsmen, descendants of the same father), though they come out of the loins of Abraham' — both the tithing Levites and the tithed people descended from Abraham, the Levites' right to tithe resting on the law, not on any superiority of nature, for they and their brethren shared one ancestor (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: they that are of the sons of Levi... have a commandment to take tithes of the people according to the law
that is, of their brethren, though they come out of the loins of Abraham
the Levites taking tithes of their brethren by law
the right resting on law, not superior nature

schools.summary: The superiority shown: 'but he whose descent is not counted from them (Melchizedek, not of Levi's line, outside the family) received tithes of Abraham (not of mere brethren, but of the patriarch himself), and blessed him that had the promises (Abraham, the recipient of God's great promises)' — Melchizedek, a stranger to the Levitical line, taking tithes from Abraham himself (the father of the Levites, a greater dignity than receiving them from his descendants) and blessing even Abraham the heir of the promises; the one who blesses being greater than the one blessed (v.7), so Melchizedek's two acts show his surpassing dignity (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: he whose descent is not counted from them received tithes of Abraham, and blessed him that had the promises
Melchizedek tithing and blessing Abraham
greater than the patriarch
the blesser greater than the blessed

schools.summary: The undisputed rule the argument rests on: 'and without all contradiction (beyond dispute, indisputably) the less (the inferior) is blessed of (by) the better (the superior)' — a recognized principle that the one who gives a blessing is greater than the one who receives it; the bearing being that since Melchizedek blessed Abraham (v.6), and the blesser is greater than the blessed, Melchizedek is greater than Abraham, and so greater than Levi and the Levitical priesthood descended from Abraham; blessing being (in the patriarchal sense) an exercise of superior authority (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Chrysostom).

themes: And without all contradiction the less is blessed of the better
the principle that the less is blessed by the greater
the blesser greater than the blessed
clinching Melchizedek's superiority

schools.summary: The further point: 'and here (in the Levitical system) men that die receive tithes' — the Levitical priests who take tithes are mortal men who die and pass on their office, their priesthood marked by death and succession; 'but there (in the case of Melchizedek) he receiveth them, of whom it is witnessed (by the silence of the record, no death being mentioned) that he liveth' — Melchizedek presented as one who lives, the record bearing witness by its silence to a continuing, undying priesthood; this living quality (in the record) making him a type of Christ, the living priest who abides for ever, the contrast foreshadowing that between the mortal Levitical priesthood and Christ's eternal one (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: here men that die receive tithes; but there he receiveth them, of whom it is witnessed that he liveth
the dying Levites and the living Melchizedek
the undying priesthood by the record's silence
a type of Christ the living priest

schools.summary: The writer's guarded statement: 'and as I may so say (hos epos eipein — so to speak, a qualifying phrase that softens a bold assertion), Levi also, who receiveth tithes, payed tithes in Abraham' — in a sense, even Levi (the ancestor of the tithe-receiving priests) paid tithes to Melchizedek through his forefather Abraham; the 'as I may so say' marking that this is a manner of speaking, not a literal act of Levi, the writer guarding the bold figure; Levi, being yet unborn but contained in Abraham (v.10), reckoned to have paid tithes representatively when Abraham did, so that the priesthood itself, in its father, acknowledged Melchizedek's superiority (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: as I may so say, Levi also, who receiveth tithes, payed tithes in Abraham
Levi himself paid tithes in Abraham
the priesthood owning Melchizedek's superiority in its father
the representative tithe

schools.summary: The ground of the previous verse: 'for he (Levi) was yet in the loins of his father (Abraham), when Melchisedec met him' — Levi not yet born but existing seminally in Abraham, his great-grandfather, present in him when the tithe was paid; the principle of seminal or representative existence (the descendant regarded as contained in the ancestor, the head acting for the posterity yet unborn), so that the whole Levitical priesthood, contained in Abraham, paid homage to Melchizedek — proving that Melchizedek's priesthood (and Christ's, after his order) is superior to the Levitical (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: For he was yet in the loins of his father, when Melchisedec met him
Levi yet in Abraham's loins when Melchizedek met him
seminal existence in the ancestor
the priesthood's homage proving Melchizedek's superiority

schools.summary: The reasoning: 'if therefore perfection (teleiosis — completeness, the full bringing of men to God, complete reconciliation and access) were by the Levitical priesthood' — if the Levitical system could have made men perfect, accomplishing full salvation, there would be no need of another; '(for under it the people received the law)' — the whole Mosaic law bound up with the Levitical priesthood, the two standing or falling together; 'what further need was there that another priest should rise after the order of Melchisedec, and not be called after the order of Aaron?' — the fact that God promised another priest of a different order (Psalm 110:4) proving the Levitical priesthood imperfect and insufficient (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: If therefore perfection were by the Levitical priesthood... what further need was there that another priest should rise after the order of Melchisedec
and not be called after the order of Aaron
the imperfection of the Levitical priesthood
another priest needed proves the old insufficient

schools.summary: The inference: 'for the priesthood being changed (transferred, from Aaron's line to Melchizedek's order), there is made of necessity a change (metathesis — alteration, transfer) also of the law' — since the law and the priesthood were bound together (v.11), to change the priesthood is necessarily to change the law; the priesthood having been taken from the Levitical order and given to Christ after the order of Melchizedek (no small thing, for the whole legal system rested on the priesthood), the ceremonial law inseparably tied to that priesthood is abrogated or transformed with it, the old covenant order passing with the priesthood on which it stood (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: For the priesthood being changed, there is made of necessity a change also of the law
a change of priesthood requires a change of law
priesthood and law inseparably linked
the old order passing with the priesthood

schools.summary: The argument from Christ's tribe: 'for he of whom these things are spoken (Christ, the new priest) pertaineth to (belongs to, is a member of) another tribe, of which no man gave attendance at the altar' — Christ came from a tribe that had no part in the priesthood, no member of his tribe ever serving at the altar; his priesthood, belonging to a non-priestly tribe (Judah, v.14), is necessarily a different priesthood from the Levitical (for under the law only Levites could serve), breaking the Levitical pattern and proving the change of priesthood and so of law (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: he of whom these things are spoken pertaineth to another tribe, of which no man gave attendance at the altar
Christ belonged to another tribe than the priestly one
a priesthood outside the Levitical line
proving the change of priesthood and law

schools.summary: The plain truth: 'for it is evident (manifest, beyond dispute) that our Lord sprang out of Juda (the tribe of Judah)' — Christ's descent from Judah, the royal tribe (the line of David), undisputed and well known, his royalty being of Judah and his priesthood not of Levi; 'of which tribe Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood' — the law assigning the priesthood to Levi alone and saying nothing of priests from Judah, so Christ's priesthood, from Judah, lies wholly outside the Mosaic provision, of a different and higher order (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: it is evident that our Lord sprang out of Juda
of which tribe Moses spake nothing concerning priesthood
Christ sprang from Judah, a non-priestly tribe
his priesthood outside the Mosaic provision

schools.summary: The heightened argument: 'and it is yet far more evident (more abundantly clear): for that after the similitude (likeness, pattern) of Melchisedec there ariseth (rises up, comes on the scene) another (heteros — of a different kind) priest' — the change of priesthood even more plainly shown by the rising of a new priest in the likeness of Melchizedek (royal, personal, perpetual), not of Aaron's hereditary Levitical pattern; not merely another in succession but a priest of a wholly different order, whose very arising confirms that the Levitical order has given way (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: it is yet far more evident: for that after the similitude of Melchisedec there ariseth another priest
another priest arising after the likeness of Melchizedek
a priest of a different order
the Levitical order giving way

schools.summary: The contrast: Christ 'is made (constituted priest) not after the law of a carnal commandment (sarkines — a fleshly, external commandment, the legal rule of physical descent that constituted the Levitical priests, requiring descent from Aaron, an outward rule based on lineage, mortality, and ceremony, earthly and transient); but after the power of an endless (akatalytos — indissoluble, indestructible) life' — his priesthood resting not on lineage or law but on the power of an imperishable life, perpetual and unfailing because he lives for ever, having conquered death (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ is priest by the power of an indestructible life; because he ever lives, his priesthood cannot pass to another or fail, unlike the mortal Levitical priests whose office ended at death — the endless life is the very ground and power of his eternal priesthood.

themes: Who is made, not after the law of a carnal commandment, but after the power of an endless life
Christ made priest by the power of an endless life
not fleshly descent but indestructible life
the unfailing priesthood

schools.summary: The Scripture cited (Psalm 110:4), the foundation of the whole argument: 'for he (God, in the Psalm) testifieth, Thou art a priest for ever (eis ton aiona — perpetually, eternally) after the order of Melchisedec' — God's own word declaring the Messiah a perpetual priest of the Melchizedek order; the key word 'for ever' marking the eternal, unending priesthood that distinguishes it from the Levitical (which ended at death) and is proved by Christ's endless life (v.16) — the everlasting priesthood the heart of the matter (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the priesthood is 'after the order of Melchisedec' — royal, personal, perpetual, not the hereditary Aaronic; Christ fulfils the Psalm as the eternal priest-king after Melchizedek's pattern, his priesthood enduring for ever.

themes: For he testifieth, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec
the testimony of an eternal priesthood after Melchizedek
the everlasting priesthood of Psalm 110:4
the eternal priest-king

schools.summary: The consequence of the new priesthood: 'for there is verily a disannulling (athetesis — a setting aside, abrogation, annulment) of the commandment going before (the former commandment, the Mosaic law of the priesthood and the ceremonial system bound to it) for the weakness (it could not give life or strength) and unprofitableness (it could not accomplish salvation or bring men to God) thereof' — the old commandment set aside, repealed, by the coming of the new priesthood; not that the law was evil, but that it was weak and could not perfect, pointing to but not providing the reality now come in Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: there is verily a disannulling of the commandment going before for the weakness and unprofitableness thereof
the setting aside of the weak old commandment
the law annulled for its inability to perfect
pointing to but not providing the reality

schools.summary: The reason for the law's abrogation: 'for the law made nothing perfect (brought nothing to completion, accomplished no full reconciliation or access to God)' — the Mosaic law, with its sacrifices and priesthood, unable to perfect the worshipper, give a clear conscience, or take away sin, preparatory not final (its work to reveal sin and point forward); 'but the bringing in (epeisagoge — introduction) of a better hope did (perfect what the law could not), by the which we draw nigh (engizomen — approach, have access) unto God' — the better hope, grounded in Christ's eternal priesthood and superseding the law, giving the real access to God the law could not, so that through it the believer draws near (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: For the law made nothing perfect, but the bringing in of a better hope did
by the which we draw nigh unto God
the law making nothing perfect, replaced by a better hope
real access to God through the better hope

schools.summary: The new point of superiority: 'and inasmuch as not without an oath he was made priest' — Christ's priesthood established with an oath (Psalm 110:4, 'the Lord sware'), unlike the Levitical priests who were made priests simply by the law's appointment, without any oath; the oath marking a higher dignity and showing that Christ's priesthood is more firmly established and more enduring than the Levitical, God's oath guaranteeing its perpetuity and superiority (developed in v.22) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: And inasmuch as not without an oath he was made priest
Christ made priest with an oath, unlike the Levites
the oath marking greater dignity
God's oath guaranteeing the priesthood

schools.summary: The comparison drawn out: 'for those priests (the Levitical) were made without an oath; but this (Christ) with an oath by him that said unto him, The Lord sware and will not repent (will not change his mind, will not revoke it), Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec' — the Levites having no oath, but Christ's priesthood sworn by God himself with an irrevocable oath; God will never retract or alter it, so the priesthood it establishes is unchangeable and eternal, the content being the everlasting Melchizedek priesthood (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: God's irrevocable oath ('will not repent') guarantees that Christ's priesthood — 'a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec' — will never pass away; the sworn perpetuity sets it infinitely above the oath-less, temporary Levitical priesthood.

themes: those priests were made without an oath; but this with an oath
The Lord sware and will not repent, Thou art a priest for ever after the order of Melchisedec
the oath confirming Christ's eternal priesthood
God's irrevocable oath guaranteeing the office

schools.summary: The result of the oath: 'by so much (in proportion to the superiority just shown, the oath) was Jesus made a surety (egguos — a guarantor, one who pledges and secures) of a better testament (diatheke — covenant)' — because his priesthood is confirmed by oath and eternal, Jesus is the guarantor of a better covenant than the old; the 'surety' being the one who guarantees the new covenant, securing and making good its promises by his own person and work (an office Jesus alone holds); the covenant 'better' than the old, founded on better promises, a better priesthood, and a better hope (the first mention of the 'better covenant' the following chapters develop) — Barnes, Robertson, Calvin.

themes: By so much was Jesus made a surety of a better testament
Jesus made the surety of a better covenant
the guarantor securing the covenant's promises
a better covenant on better promises

schools.summary: The contrast being drawn: 'and they truly were many priests (a long succession of high priests over the centuries), because they were not suffered to continue (prevented from continuing in office) by reason of death' — the Levitical priests many because death removed each in turn and a successor had to take his place; the high-priesthood passing through many mortal hands, none continuing, their very mortality the cause of the continual change and the mark of the weakness of the Levitical priesthood over against the abiding Christ (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: they truly were many priests, because they were not suffered to continue by reason of death
the many Levitical priests, ended by death
a long succession of mortal priests
mortality marking the priesthood's weakness

schools.summary: The great difference: 'but this man (Christ), because he continueth ever (abides for ever, never dying), hath an unchangeable (aparabatos — that does not pass to another, untransferable, permanent) priesthood' — unlike the dying Levites whose priesthood passed from one to the next, Christ lives for ever (his endless life, v.16, the ground), so his priesthood never passes to a successor but is held by him alone, inviolably and perpetually (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: But this man, because he continueth ever, hath an unchangeable priesthood
Christ's unchangeable priesthood, because he continues forever
an untransferable, permanent priesthood
never passing to a successor

schools.summary: The glorious conclusion: 'wherefore he is able (has the power) also to save them to the uttermost (eis to panteles — completely, perfectly, to the very end, and for all time) that come unto God by him' — because his priesthood is eternal, Christ can save completely and for ever all who approach God through him, nothing lacking in his salvation (whether 'to the uttermost' is taken in degree, completely, or in time, for ever, his salvation is total and final); 'seeing he ever liveth (lives perpetually) to make intercession (entynchanein — to plead, intercede, appear on their behalf) for them' — his unending life and ceaseless intercession at God's right hand the ground of his complete saving power, so their salvation is secure (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the living priest ever pleads for his people; because he abides for ever and never ceases to intercede, those who come to God through him are saved to the uttermost — his perpetual life and advocacy the unfailing security of their salvation.

themes: he is able also to save them to the uttermost that come unto God by him
seeing he ever liveth to make intercession for them
Christ able to save to the uttermost, ever living to intercede
his endless life and intercession the ground of salvation

schools.summary: The suitability: 'for such an high priest became (was fitting for, suited) us' — a high priest of this character exactly what our need required, his perfections answering our necessities; the fourfold description of his sinlessness: 'holy (hosios — pious, devoted to God), harmless (akakos — innocent, free from malice or guile), undefiled (amiantos — pure, unstained by sin), separate from sinners (set apart, morally distinct from sinful men)' — Christ's perfect sinlessness, unlike the Levitical priests who had to offer for their own sins (v.27); 'and made higher than the heavens (exalted above all heavens, to God's right hand)' — his exaltation matching his purity (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Henry).

themes: such an high priest became us, who is holy, harmless, undefiled, separate from sinners
and made higher than the heavens
the sinless, exalted high priest fitted to our need
perfect purity and supreme exaltation

schools.summary: The difference from the Levitical priests: 'who needeth not daily (the 'daily' covering the regular recurring sacrifices, with reference also to the high priest's yearly Day of Atonement offering), as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's' — the Levitical high priest, being himself a sinner, having to offer first for his own sins before he could offer for the people, and the sacrifices repeated again and again, never finally taking away sin (the repetition proving their insufficiency); 'for this he did once (hapax — once for all), when he offered up himself' — Christ, needing no sacrifice for his own sins (being sinless), offering not an animal but himself, and that once for all, his single perfect self-offering accomplishing what the repeated Levitical sacrifices could not (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: Christ offered himself once for all — the sinless priest who is also the spotless victim; his single self-offering, needing no repetition and no offering for himself, perfectly accomplishes the atonement the repeated Levitical sacrifices could only foreshadow.

themes: who needeth not daily, as those high priests, to offer up sacrifice, first for his own sins, and then for the people's
for this he did once, when he offered up himself
Christ offering himself once, unlike the repeated sacrifices
the once-for-all self-offering

schools.summary: The summary contrast: 'for the law maketh (appoints) men high priests which have infirmity (astheneian — weakness, moral and physical frailty, including sinfulness and mortality)' — the law appointing weak, sinful, mortal men; 'but the word of the oath (Psalm 110:4, God's sworn declaration), which was since (after, later than) the law, maketh the Son (not weak men, but the Son of God), who is consecrated (teteleiomenon — perfected, made complete, fully qualified as priest) for evermore (for ever)' — the oath, coming after the law and superseding it, appointing the eternal Son, perfected through his sufferings (2:10; 5:9) and consecrated priest for ever; the contrast complete — weak men by the law, the perfected Son by the oath (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

Cyril: the oath appoints the Son, consecrated and perfected for evermore — not a frail, mortal man but the eternal Son, fully qualified as priest through his sufferings and abiding for ever; the later word of the oath crowns and supersedes the law's weak priesthood.

themes: the law maketh men high priests which have infirmity
the word of the oath, which was since the law, maketh the Son, who is consecrated for evermore
the law's weak priests, the oath's perfected Son
the eternal high priest perfected for ever
